figures.caption: Table 1: Comparison on RealEstate10k dataset under varying number of input views. During training, all models take two views as input. α−X denotes applying alpha normalization to the X method at inference time without retraining. Alpha normalization shows consistent improvements.

Figure 1: Left: DepthSplat [23] exhibits hole artifacts due to its predicted small-scale Gaussians. Our method mitigate the hole issues and produce more complete view. Right: Results overview: As the number of input views and rendering resolution increase, our method consistently achieves better image quality than DepthSplat.
Figure 5: Qualitative results on RealEstate10K. Integrating alpha normalization helps to mitigate the overbrightness issues encountered by [6, 23] when increasing the number of input views.
Table 4: Ablation study on DL3DV. Integrating alpha normalization, 3D-sampling based regularizer improve the image quality under varying input view and high-resolution rendering, respectively.
Figure 6: High-resolution rendering on DL3DV dataset. Our method significantly alleviates the holes issues exhibited by DepthSplat [23].

Figure 2: Overall pipeline. We introduce two components into the existing pixel-wise Gaussian prediction framework. First, alpha normalization is integrated into the rendering process to improve robustness to varying numbers of input views. Second, a 3D sampling-based regularizer L3D promotes accurate Gaussian scale estimation, mitigating hole artifacts under high-resolution rendering.
Figure 3: Alpha normalization adjusts each Gaussian’s contribution based on its overlap count to avoid overbright rendering when increasing the number of input views.
Figure 7: Ablation study on DL3DV. Alpha normalization improves the robustness to varying input views, while 3D sampling-based regularizer improves high-resolution rendering.
